summary: The branch implements the backlog artifact and most of the merge-gate wording, but the new close-step status lifecycle still has a merge-path bug: the `merged` status commit is made before the merge and is not guaranteed to be included in the remote PR merge.

findings.title: Remote merge path can omit the merged-status commit
findings.claim: Step 5 creates the `Status: merged` commit locally at line 25, then the remote path runs `gh pr merge --merge --delete-branch` without any instruction to push or otherwise update the PR head first. That means the merge command is not guaranteed to include the new status commit, contradicting line 30's claim that the `Status: merged` line arrives with the merge and violating AC1/AC4's no-separate-base-commit status lifecycle.
findings.suggestion: After the status commit, push the feature branch and verify the PR head/checks are on that SHA before `gh pr merge`, or use a merge-state strategy that does not depend on an unpublished pre-merge commit.

findings.title: Merged status is still committed before the merge happens
findings.claim: The hard constraint at line 14 says never assert `Status: merged` before the merge has actually happened and that status/reality must agree at every observable point, but step 5 still tells `/close` to set and commit `Status: merged` before issuing the merge command. If the merge prompts, hangs, is interrupted, or fails before the revert step completes, the branch again has a committed `merged` header while unmerged, which is the D1/AC4 failure this story is intended to prevent.
findings.suggestion: Do not commit a final `merged` header before the merge command. Keep `approved`/`ready` until the merge is complete, record merge state via merge/PR metadata, or explicitly accept and document a trailing post-merge status commit instead.
